# mzgb Benchmarks

Comparison of **mzgb v0.3** vs `grep` and `ripgrep` on a 302 MB synthetic log file (4 million lines).

**Hardware:** Apple Silicon (ARM64), macOS Sequoia  
**File:** 4,000,000 lines, 302 MB — generated by `benchmarks/gen_logs.py`  
**Tool:** [hyperfine](https://github.com/sharkdp/hyperfine) — 2 warmup + 5 runs each

## Setup

```bash
# Install tools
brew install hyperfine ripgrep

# Generate benchmark file (~300 MB, 4M lines)
python3 benchmarks/gen_logs.py

# Run
bash benchmarks/run.sh
```

## Results

### Level filter + Pattern search (all commands)

| Command | Mean [s] | Min [s] | Max [s] | Relative |
|:---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| `mzgb --level ERROR` | 139.63 ± 15.67 | 117.69 | 152.17 | 951.9× |
| `grep ERROR` | 5.09 ± 0.51 | 4.22 | 5.52 | 34.7× |
| `rg ERROR` | 0.273 ± 0.021 | 0.248 | 0.296 | 1.86× |
| `mzgb --pattern timeout` | 115.01 ± 0.93 | 114.40 | 116.63 | 784.1× |
| `grep timeout` | 5.79 ± 0.07 | 5.71 | 5.89 | 39.5× |
| `rg timeout` | **0.147 ± 0.005** | 0.142 | 0.154 | **1.00×** (fastest) |

> **Honest note:** mzgb is significantly slower than grep/ripgrep at raw scan speed.
> This is because mzgb does more work per line: full log parsing (JSON/logfmt/plaintext
> detection, timestamp normalization, field extraction) before matching.
> grep/rg scan raw bytes; mzgb builds a structured `LogLine` for every line.
>
> mzgb's value is in **structured filtering** — combining `--level`, `--pattern`,
> `--from`, `--to`, `--context`, `--summary` and Drain3 clustering in one pass
> over logs that cannot be processed by grep at all.

## Engine Selection (v0.3)

mzgb v0.3 automatically selects the fastest available matching engine:

| Pattern type | Engine | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Single literal (no regex metacharacters) | **Boyer-Moore** (`str.find`) | Python's `str.find` uses Boyer-Moore-Horspool internally |
| Single regex pattern | **Bloom + regex** | Trigram Bloom gate before `re.search` (requires `pybloom-live`) |
| N ≥ 2 literal patterns | **Aho-Corasick** | O(n) automaton via `pyahocorasick` (requires `mzgb[fast]`) |
| N ≥ 2 regex patterns | **Multi-regex** | Single compiled alternation `(?:p1)\|(?:p2)\|…` |
| No pattern | **Always** | Zero matching overhead |

Install optional engines:

```bash
pip install "mzgb[fast]"   # Aho-Corasick + Bloom filter
pip install "mzgb[drain]"  # Drain3 template clustering
pip install "mzgb[all]"    # Everything
```

## Per-stage Timing (`--bench`)

```bash
mzgb --bench --pattern timeout benchmarks/bench.log > /dev/null
```

```
bench  matcher engine : literal
bench  total    : 115,014 ms
bench  parse    :  98,201 ms
bench  match    :   3,412 ms
bench  lines/s  :  34,769
```

The parse stage dominates (~85% of time) — format detection, timestamp normalization,
and `LogLine` construction. The match stage itself is fast; Boyer-Moore on 4M lines
takes only ~3.4 s.
